pieces.text: iran has turned the strait of hormuz into something close to a toll booth, demanding a dollar per barrel from any ship that wants to pass, payable in bitcoin or chinese yuan. the world's biggest oil chokepoint is basically being run like a privatised border crossing.

the imf will downgrade its global growth forecast when it meets in washington this week, and the managing director has said the damage will be permanent even if the war ends tomorrow. when the world's top economic referee uses the word permanent, that's worth sitting with.

qatar supplies about a fifth of the world's liquefied natural gas, and it has told all its customers it simply cannot deliver right now. that's left japan, south korea, china and india scrambling for fuel at prices that more than doubled almost overnight.

germany spent years following strict rules about how much it could borrow, then last month threw those rules out and approved half a trillion euros for infrastructure and defence. it's the biggest spending shift in a generation, and it tells you how seriously europe is taking the new world it finds itself in.

india is still the fastest-growing large economy on earth, projected to expand at 6.6% this year, even with higher energy costs eating into household and business budgets. the middle east crisis is a headwind, but india is far enough from the epicentre that it keeps moving.

a ceasefire was announced on april 8th and the dow jumped over 1,300 points in a single day, one of its best days in almost a year. by the next morning the strait was still closed and the gains had vanished, which is a pretty clean picture of how much uncertainty is baked into everything right now.
